cards.front: What is the stability question really asking?
cards.back: It asks whether the system response settles down, stays bounded, or grows after a disturbance.

cards.front: What are the roots of the denominator polynomial called?
What time-domain factor comes from a real pole $p$?
cards.back: A real pole contributes a term proportional to $e^{pt}$.

cards.front: What time-domain form comes from a complex pole pair $\sigma \pm j\omega$?
cards.back: It contributes a decaying or growing oscillation such as $e^{\sigma t}\cos(\omega t + \theta)$.

cards.front: What is the asymptotic stability condition in pole language?
Why does the left half plane correspond to decay?
cards.back: Because poles there have negative real part, so the exponential envelope shrinks with time.
No. Zeros shape the response, but the poles decide whether the natural modes decay or grow.

cards.front: What does a right-half-plane pole imply?
cards.back: It creates a growing exponential or growing oscillation, so the system is unstable.
It means the natural transient response dies away, so the system forgets the disturbance over time.

cards.front: How should you attack a stability question from $H(s)$?
cards.back: Find the poles, inspect their real parts, connect them to the forms in $h(t)$, then classify the system.
